author: @nameismetatoo4591
text:
Great idea with the supercapacitor. I think we're going to start seeing them used more often in regen braking systems as their price goes down and their capacity goes up. They're perfectly suited for capturing large current spikes and frequent charge/discharge cycles (not to mention their superior safety over Lithium batteries).

You should try experimenting with putting multiple supercapacitors on the output, and test their efficiency in parallel vs series (or combinations of the two). It would be easier to test the efficiency by feeding the power back into the motor after all the usable energy is recovered, then measuring the maximum speed of the flywheel after discharging.

author: @tmathison66
text:
It is utterly impossible to ever, with maybe two exceptions, to read the numbers on that multimeter - at least on a phone. I can't tell you how frustrating it is for me to watch you read the numbers and not do it out loud, while getting giddy about how impressed we might be without any idea what the voltage and amp ranges we're dealing with are, unless you happen to mention both of them.

Half the time you give us anything it's either volts or amps but not both. Also frustrating because both matter and simultaneous numbers matter too because of variance between attempts. (pedal, hand crank, twist, etc.)

People have told you this. You acknowledged it at least a few times. You could pay next to nothing to get simultaneous display of amps and volts that could be visible in your videos. I would say it's about time wouldn't you? We could learn more and focus our own ideas better if you did.

I hadn't quite completed the video before posting the above. you actually wrapped that one up satisfactorily with "disappointing" and "couple of volts couple of amps".

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: seems more of a kinetic battery you are building, better bearings and it would keep that energy for quite a long time waiting for you to engage the motor and extract that as electricity, a kinetic recovery system would be more like the magnetic breaking in an electric vehicle that uses the energy you throw away to say stop at a red light to fire off the car again when the light is green instead of taking more energy you bought (gas at gas station) to get back up to speed while the removal of it to stop is not only lost but you are throwing away break pads thus it's costing you both in wasted gasoline energy AND break pad material that eventually you have to buy new pads from constantly grinding them down by the stopping, as well as replacing rotors too from grinding them down too, though much slower as they are usually much harder material so the pad sacrifices material primarily, in the magnetic breaking you turn off the coils connection to the battery and switch those connections over to a charge unit to allow the magnets flying past to generate eddy currents causing resistance (cogging) and the electrons flowing to cause this are captured by the charge unit and shoved into a super cap or battery so you can extract it later when you switch those connections back to motor position so you can pulse it through the coils forcing the magnets to move rather than draggingg down on them slowing them down as they do in charge/breaking action,  TLDR of that is that there is no physical contact so nothing is physically being abraded by this action to slow your vehicle down and you save money two ways, :)

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: I have actually been planning something like this using the armature magnets in hard drives for a motor by putting them on the wheel's hub, and putting coils on a stator right next to them hooked up to a pulse controller so they can be used like a bi-directional brushless motor, and then figure out even if it has to be one of those physical switches that use a coil to move the switch from bridging contact set A to contact set B such as used in emergency back up battery systems for large units, where the coil is acting as a solenoid off the mains AC pulling the switch fingers down, and when the AC goes out, the fingers spring up touching a secondary set of contacts together that bring the battery powered inverter system into contact with the household or business's power lines running to the outlets and all the appliances and lights, but with the solenoid in this case hooked up to a switch that the break pedal activates and pushing further down on it causes the coil stator to move closer to the magnets inducing more eddy current into them, or has a pot resistor hooked up lowering the resistance between coils and charge unit or something though using arduino and hooking it up to that pot as analog input to control everything with solid state now seems more the direction to go,

author: @MrAnderson4509
text: Hey Luke, I modified that motor you used in your scooter, there was too much resistance under load for me, the delivery wires got hot. I disassembled it and found 4 brush points around the commutator, I rewired the inside to 4 separate power leads of a bit heavier gage,  and installed some 300 volt electrolytic condensers in non polarized configuration across the power leads to lower the arcing on commutator help with loss and temperature. I also ported the end cap in between the brushes to help with the poor air flow. then it was run over voltage by twice, a nice addition would have been a dc squirrel cage cooling fan to run the air through the commentator brush area to help keep the temperature down, although it didn't need it in the race, have fun👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Luke doesn't read the comments on videos he isn't in mate - but I will let him know - nice advice - cheers

author: @OverlandOne
text: I really enjoy watching your videos.  You always create such interesting projects and explain them very well.  I believe the fly wheel is also a battery for storing mechanical energy.  Many years ago (1960's I believe) they were experimenting on running transit busses on huge, heavy flywheels.  They were designed to use a much smaller engine than was usually used in busses because, all it had to do was run at a constant speed where it was the most efficient and the bus driver simply engaged a clutch and gearbox to tap into the energy stored in this flywheel.  When the bus was stopped like at a red light, the engine had plenty of time to get the flywheel back up to max. speed.  The flywheel, of course, did not add any energy to the system, it only stored it but, it took advantage of the time factor.  The bus could wait a few minutes, when first started in the morning, to get the flywheel up to speed before driving off so the small engine was simply using more time to store the same energy that could be used to operate the bus that the larger engine provided.  From what I remember, the prototype worked great but it obviously never went into production.  Maybe because, as I remember, gas was like $.15/gallon then?  (Diesel was also cheaper than gas was back then too.)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there was a lot of interest in alternatives back in the 70s due to the oil crisis - some great electric cars where developed then

replies.author: @strongforce8466
replies.text: yea look up gyrobus I was surprised when I first heard about this

replies.author: @OverlandOne
replies.text: @strongforce8466 1940's...nice.  That is earlier than the one I remember reading about when I was in school in the mid 1960's.  I think the US one used a small gas engine to spin up the flywheel but it is amazing the Swiss did this so long ago.  Thanks for the info.

replies.author: @strongforce8466
replies.text:
@OverlandOne  you're welcome, yeah I was quite astonished when I heard about it, though when you think about it it's nothing too crazy technologically speaking, I was also quite surprised to hear about electric cars in the 1800s or something !!  

One can wonder if only people/companies/governements had focused on these type of tech early on, we wouldn't be in the mess that we're in ! 

If a single old system with motor/flywheel could run 5-10 KM can you imagine if this stuff was refined, you add multiple flywheels..with better efficiency of today's engineering

It's kind of the story about solar panels and that in the 60-70s (not sure exactly don't quote me on that) but they were already developing the first solar cells back then, imagine if instead of focusing on nuclear factories they researched more the solar cells the solar panels we would have today would probably make centralized energy creation with huge factories useless (Oh I'm sure the nuclear / oil / gas lobbies wouldn't be so happy about it though, the electrical/solar cells ones of course lol)

replies.author: @OverlandOne
replies.text: @strongforce8466 I have one of the first production solar cells ever made.  My Dad worked at Bell Labs in the 1950's and 1960's and these were developed for the Telstar (1st communications satellite) which was launched in 1962.  The cell I have was made earlier than that...probably around 59 or 60.  It still works but is very low power which was considered very high power for a cell back then.
